segments.s400-g73: به + indirect object: 'به ترکان همه گفت' = 'he said to all the Turks,' with به marking the addressees and گفت in the simple past (3rd sg.). 'بیرون شوید' is a plural jussive/imperative built with شوید (subjunctive/imperative of شدن) and the adverb بیرون 'out' — here functioning as a command 'go out.'
segments.s400-g74: ز دژ = 'from the fortress' (ز is the poetic/directional 'from'). یکسره 'straightaway/without delay' modifies the following motion. 'سوی هامون شوید' = 'turn/go toward the plain,' with سوی marking direction, هامون 'the plain,' and شوید again the plural jussive/imperative.
segments.s400-g75: همه لشکر 'the whole army' is the direct object; اندر میان = 'into the midst/center' (اندر = in/within, میان = middle). آورید is the plural imperative/jussive of آوردن 'bring' or 'put in.'
segments.s400-g76: خروش هژبر ژیان is a noun phrase where خروش 'cry/roar' is qualified by هژبر (an epithet 'mighty/valiant') and ژیان 'of life' or 'life-giving'; آورید is again the plural imperative 'produce/let out.' Together: 'let out the mighty, life-giving shout.'
segments.s400-g77: یکی زنده زیشان 'not one alive among them' — یکی = 'one' (here 'not a single one'), زنده = 'alive,' زیشان = 'of them/among them.' ممانید is a negative jussive/imperative meaning 'do not remain/stay' (2nd pl.). نیز adds emphasis 'also/indeed.'
segments.s400-g78: کسی نام ایشان مخوانید نیز = 'let no one call/utter their name either.' کسی here functions in the sense 'anyone/no one' in this negative command, نام 'name,' ایشان 'their/them,' and مخوانید (here) = 'do not call/summon' (imperative/jussive negative). نیز again adds 'also/likewise.'
segments.s400-g79: همه لشکر 'the entire army' is subject; از دژ 'from the fortress' (از = from), به راه 'onto the road' (به marking direction), آمدند is past plural 'they came' (3rd pl.).
segments.s400-g80: جگر خسته و کینه‌خواه آمدند describes the manner: جگر here = 'heart/seat of feeling' (poetic for 'heart/soul'), خسته = 'weary,' کینه‌خواه = 'seeking vengeance/avenger.' آمدند repeats 'they came,' so the clause means 'they came with weary hearts and as avengers.'
segments.s400-g25: بفرمود is an honorific/past reportive verb meaning 'he ordered/said' (3rd‑person past). تا here is a complementizer 'that' introducing the subordinate clause. کهرم functions as a proper name/title in apposition, and شیرگیر is a descriptive epithet 'lion‑catcher' attached to it; the sequence تا + [name + epithet] introduces the person who is to act.
segments.s400-g26: برد is used as a verbal predicate meaning 'take/lead' in this poetic/past sense (the supplied lexical senses did not list this verbal reading). The following nouns form a coordinated list of objects (لشکر و کوس و شمشیر و تیر) joined by repeated و; وتیر is written as a fused form meaning 'and‑arrow'.
segments.s400-g27: به طرخان places the indirect object before the verb: 'to/for Tarkhan.' چنین is an adverb 'thus' and گفت is the 3rd‑person past 'he said' introducing direct speech. کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing the addressee, followed by سرفراز as an epithet 'exalted / proud one.'
segments.s400-g28: برو is the imperative 'go.' تیز here specifies manner 'swift(ly).' با + لشکری means 'with an army/force' (لشکری is an attributive/collective form). رزمساز is an adjectival/epithet applied to the force (literally 'war‑making' / 'fit for battle'), placed after the noun in this poetic noun‑phrase.
segments.s400-g29: ببر is an imperative 'take!/seize!' (poetic/archaic). نامدران here functions as the name or identifier of the place/people connected to the following noun, and دژ is the direct object 'fortress.' The numeral phrase ده هزار (ten thousand) follows to specify the force size — poetic word order permits the number after the object.
segments.s400-g30: همه 'all' quantifies the group; رزم + جویان forms the compound رزمجویان 'battle‑seekers / fighters' (جویان is the deverbal/agentive element 'seekers'). خنجرگزار is an epithet 'dagger‑bearer' modifying those fighters, placed after them as a descriptive compound.
segments.s400-g31: نگه کن is an imperative 'look!/watch!' formed from نگه (root/noun 'look') + the imperative verb کن. که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that / how.' این جنگجویان is the topical subject 'these warriors,' and کیند is an archaic 3rd‑plural form meaning 'they do/are' used here to assert what they are or what they do.
segments.s400-g32: وزین qualifies the following verbal noun/infinitive and means 'weighty/grave' (i.e. forceful or heavy). تاختن is 'to charge/gallop' and together وزین تاختن conveys 'a heavy/forceful charge.' ساختن here functions as a catenative verb 'to contrive/make (to...)' and is followed by برچیند, an embedded verb meaning 'to clear away / pluck off'; the sequence expresses that, by that charge, they set about clearing or cutting down the foe.
segments.s400-g9: پشوتن چنین گفت introduces reported speech: پشوتن is the speaker's proper name, چنین 'thus/so' modifies گفت 'said'. کز is an archaic fused form of که + ز here functioning as 'that' to introduce the content clause; پیل و شیر are two nouns listed in coordination (elephant and lion) and form the beginning of that content clause.
segments.s400-g10: This is the continuation of the content clause introduced by کز. فزونست is an older third‑person singular present/past‑sense verb 'is/was more' (fozun‑st), and its logical subject is مرد دلیر 'the brave man' which follows the verb (permitted word order in epic Persian). به تنبل is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the lazy' expressing the comparison/relation (i.e. 'more toward the lazy'), so literally 'the brave man is more toward the lazy' — naturally rendered 'more given to sloth'.
segments.s400-g11: که begins a clause (here carrying an optative/wishive sense). چشم 'eye' (here: sight) is the grammatical subject; the sequence ب + دان is an archaic/poetic element (here part of the phrase بدان meaning roughly 'to that' or bound into the idiom). از تنش 'from his body' gives the source/placement, دور 'far' is predicate/adverb, and باد is the optative particle 'may' expressing a wish or curse (common in classical Persian).
segments.s400-g12: This is a short optative clause: همه روزگاران 'all the days/ages' is the temporal phrase, او 'his' marks possession, سور 'feast/banquet' is a nominal predicate, and باد is again the optative 'may' (wishing/formulaic). The whole line reads as a wish/blessing (or possibly ironic formula) 'may all his days be a feast'.
segments.s400-g13: بزد is a simple past tense verb 'he struck/hit'. It takes two coordinated direct objects: نای رویین 'the iron (or iron‑clad) ney' and رویینه خم 'the iron/armored jar/urn' — adjectives (رویین/رویینه) follow the nouns in this word order common in poetry, and و links the two objects.
segments.s400-g14: برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth'. ز در is a poetic order meaning 'from the door/gate' (ز = from, در = door). ناله is the sound/cry (here 'lowing' or 'lament'), and گاودم is a compounds/poetic term relating to cattle (literally 'cow‑tail' in the glosses but used here to mark the bovine sound); together the phrase means 'the cattle's cry came forth from the gate'.
segments.s400-g15: This clause uses the poetic order z + place + verb: ز هامون 'from the plain', سوی دژ 'toward the fortress', بیامد 'came' (perfective/past), and سپاه 'an army/host' appears after the verb as its logical subject — a common word order in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s400-g16: This is a follow‑up descriptive line where شد 'became' links the army (previous noun) to the following description: از گرد خورشید تابان 'from around the shining sun' sets the visual backdrop, and سیاه 'black/dark' is the predicate adjective — together giving the image 'the host appeared dark against the shining sun'.
segments.s400-g57: چنان (fronted adverb) = 'thus/so', هم = 'also' adding emphasis/continuation from previous action, بقلب is a prepositional compound (بـ + قلب) meaning 'into the heart (of...)', سپه = 'host/army' is the object of بقلب, and حمله برد is a verbal phrase where حمله (attack) is the object and برد is the past tense verb 'carried/made' (classical literary use of بردن). The whole segment reads: 'thus also he made an attack into the heart of the host.'
segments.s400-g58: بزرگش = بزرگ + possessive clitic ش meaning 'one of his great/important (men)'; یکی بود = 'there was one' (یکی as the numeral/indefinite 'one' + بود past 'was'); با مرد خرد = prepositional phrase 'with a man of wisdom' where با = 'with', مرد = 'man', خرد = 'wisdom'. Together: 'one of his great men was a wise man.'
segments.s400-g59: بران‌سان is a simile/adverbial compound meaning 'like a charger' or 'as if charging'; دو لشکر = 'the two hosts/armies' (subject); بهم = 'upon one another/together' indicating reciprocal collision; برشکست is the past tense verbal compound (بـ + شکست) meaning 'broke/burst/struck and broke.' The clause describes two armies charging and breaking upon each other.
segments.s400-g60: که introduces a result/explanatory clause here ('so that' / 'such that'); از تیر = 'from/of arrows' (از = 'from/of', تیر = 'arrow'); بر سرکشان = 'upon their heads' (بر = 'on/upon', سرکشان = 'their heads' — here a possessive plural on سرک-); ابر بست = 'a cloud closed' (ابر = 'cloud', بست = past of بستن 'to close', i.e. 'a cloud of arrows closed over them'). The clause paints the image of a cloud (of arrows) covering their heads.
segments.s400-g61: سرافراز is an epithet meaning 'triumphant/exalted' placed before the name; کهرم here is the personal name (subject). سوی دژ = 'toward the fortress' (سوی = 'toward', دژ = 'fortress'); برفت is a literary past form (بـ + رفت) meaning 'he went' — an archaic/classical past-tense compound. Note the verb precedes or follows subject in classical order depending on emphasis; here the verbal form is past.
segments.s400-g62: گریزان is a participial/adverbial adjective meaning 'fleeing' (describing the subject's manner); و = 'and'; لشکر = 'the army' (subject of the second action); همی راند is a classical present/imperfective construction where همی marks continuous or habitual aspect and راند is the past/preterite of راندن 'drove/pursued' (here 'pressed on' or 'pursued'); تفت is a short adverbial/intensifier in context (not the culinary sense listed), functioning to add force to راند (pursued fiercely) — the listed senses for تفت do not match this poetic usage.
segments.s400-g63: چنین = 'thus/so' (signal of reported speech); گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used as main verb introducing direct speech); کهرم is the speaker's name (subject); به پیش پدر = 'in the presence of his father' (به پیش = 'before/in the presence of', پدر = 'father'). Word order is the classical narrative pattern with the reporting verb followed by the speaker and the prepositional phrase marking addressee/location.
segments.s400-g64: که‌ای here functions as an archaic/vocative exclamation ('که ای' ≈ 'O' addressing someone); نامور = 'renowned' (epithet), شاه = 'king' (title), خورشیدفر is the proper name/title being addressed. The segment is a direct vocative: 'O renowned King Khurshidfar.'
segments.s400-g65: This is a compact nominal+verb clause in classical word order: prepositional source ('از ایران' = 'from Iran') + the noun 'سپاهی' (an army/force, here treated as a collective) + the past tense verb 'بیامد' ('came'). The final adjective 'بزرگ' ('great') follows the noun it modifies, as is regular in Persian poetry.
segments.s400-g66: This phrase gives the destination/position and an epithet: 'به پیش اندرون' = 'to/towards the presence/inner quarters' (preposition + locative), followed by a two-word epithet 'نامداری سترگ' qualifying the arriving force. In older/persianate style adjectives or epithets can follow the noun or stand after a locative phrase; here 'نامداری' = 'renown' is a noun used adjectivally and 'سترگ' is an archaic poetic adjective intensifying it.
segments.s400-g67: 'سرافراز اسفندیارست' fuses the adjective 'سرافراز' ('lofty, honored') with the proper name plus copula: 'اسفندیارست' = 'اسفندیار است' ('Esfandiar is'). The short conjunction 'و' links the clause to the concluding particle 'بس' ('enough, that suffices'), a terse emphatic closing common in epic diction.
segments.s400-g68: The clause expresses prohibition/absence: the prepositional prefix 'ب' (here = 'به' = 'to/into') + the demonstrative/item (orthography in the manuscript appears as 'دین' but functions as 'این' = 'this') + 'دژ' ('fortress') give 'to this fortress'. 'نیاید' is classical negative present/subjunctive meaning 'does not come / should not come / may not enter', and 'جزو هیچ‌کس' is a poetic way to say 'not even a single person' (literally 'part/member of no one'), i.e., 'no one'. Note: some word boundaries are archaic/poetic spellings; the grammar is 'به این دژ نیاید هیچ‌کس' → 'no one may enter this fortress.'
segments.s400-g69: 'همان نیزه' = 'that very spear' (demonstrative + noun). The noun 'جنگ' immediately after 'نیزه' is poetic apposition: 'نیزهٔ جنگ' = 'spear of war' (i.e., a war-spear). 'دارد' is the present tense auxiliary 'has/possesses', and 'به چنگ' is an adverbial phrase 'in (its) grasp/hand' (literally 'to/at claw'), a vivid image meaning the spear is held or is having a fighting grip. Word order places the possessed attribute before the verb, as common in Persian.
segments.s400-g70: This relative clause begins with 'که' = 'which/that' and gives the locus where the addressee earlier saw it: 'در گنبدان دژ' = 'in/at the Gonbadān fortress' (proper name + 'fortress'), then 'تو دیدی' = 'you saw'. The final 'به جنگ' is an adverbial phrase 'in battle' (lit. 'to/for war'), linking the sighting to combat — 'which you saw at Gonbadān fortress in battle.'
segments.s400-g71: This is a caused-state construction: 'غمی شد دل ارجاسپ را' literally 'a grief became (the) heart of Arjasp' — i.e., 'Arjasp's heart was grieved.' 'شد' functions as 'became' (copular/auxiliary) and the object-marking particle 'را' attaches to the proper name to mark the affected experiencer. 'زان سخن' = 'from that speech' (the cause) follows to indicate source of the grief.
segments.s400-g72: 'که' introduces the content/explanatory clause: 'نو شد دگر باره کین کهن' = 'the old hatred became new again.' Here 'نو شد' = 'became new' (renewed), and 'دگر باره' is a pleonastic pair meaning 'once more / again'; 'کین کهن' = 'ancient/old enmity' is the thing renewed. The sequence is direct and typical of narrative: cause (that speech) → effect (old grudge revived).
segments.s400-g1: This is two coordinated clauses in poetic word order: 'شب آمد' (literally 'night came') is a simple past clause with subject before verb. The second clause 'یکی آتشی برفروخت' uses 'یکی' as an indefinite pronoun 'someone', then the object 'آتشی' (a fire) and the past verb 'برفروخت' (kindled). The verb shows the older Persian prefix 'بر-' attached to the past stem and the whole clause is a compact narrative action.
segments.s400-g2: 'که' introduces a result/explanatory clause ('whose...'). 'تفش' = 'تف + -ش' where the -ش is a possessive clitic ('its spit/flare'); here it means the fire's spitting/flare. 'همی' is the older continuous/emphatic particle (like classical 'می'), giving an ongoing/intense flavor to the action. 'آسمان را' is the definite object marked by 'را', and 'بسوخت' is the past passive/intransitive 'burned up' — together: 'whose spitting indeed burned the sky.'
segments.s400-g3: 'چو' = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. 'از دیده‌گه' is a prepositional phrase 'from the lookout/field of view' (دیده‌گه = vantage/lookout). 'دیده‌بان' is the subject 'the watchman', and 'بنگرید' is an older past-form of the verb 'to look/gaze' (with the archaic prefix 'بـ-'); syntactically it means 'when the watchman looked from the lookout.'
segments.s400-g4: This clause gives what he perceived. The preposition 'به' marks the thing regarded ('with respect to' or roughly 'as'); the construction is poetic and omits a copula — 'به شب آتش و روز پردود دید' literally lists the two contrasts 'night (as) fire and day (as) smoky' with 'دید' ('saw') final. Adjectival/nominal predicates (آتش, پردود) describe the sensory impression.
segments.s400-g5: 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from'; 'جایی' = 'somewhere' and 'که' introduces the relative/explanatory clause. 'بد شادمان' is an adjective phrase where 'بد' negates/qualifies 'شادمان' (literally 'bad-happy' = 'unhappy/sorrowful'). 'بازگشت' here is the past verb 'he returned' (verb form used without an explicit subject in the line).
segments.s400-g6: 'تو گفتی' = 'you said' (second-person past) introducing reported speech with the complementizer 'که' ('that'). The clause 'با باد همباز گشت' has 'با باد' ('with the wind'), 'همباز' (a fellow/companion/peer) as predicate nominal, and 'گشت' used as 'became'/'turned into'—so the reported content is 'he became/was a companion with the wind.' Note: the printed senses for 'گفتی' in the prompt are lexicalized oddly, so its intended meaning here is the ordinary past 'you said.'
segments.s400-g7: 'چو' = 'when' marks the temporal clause; 'از راه' = 'from the road' (indicating origin or route); 'نزد پشوتن رسید' literally 'arrived near/at Peshotan' — 'نزد' = 'near/at the presence of', 'پشوتن' is the proper name, and 'رسید' is the past 'arrived/reached.' The whole phrase sets the scene: 'when he reached Peshotan from the road.'
segments.s400-g8: 'بگفت' (he said) is an older narrative past form (prefix 'بـ-'). It takes the following noun clause as its object: 'آنچ' = 'that which/what', 'از آتش و دود دید' = 'he saw of fire and smoke' (a relative clause where 'دید' is the past verb). Put together: 'he said that which he had seen of the fire and smoke.'
segments.s400-g17: همه is the subject meaning “all/everyone.” زیر خفتان is a compact poetic phrase: زیر (under/below) + خفتان (an archaic/verbal formative from خفتن 'to sleep') here used substantively to indicate being prostrate/laid low; خود اندرون is an emphatic inward adverbial (خود = themselves/their very selves; اندرون = within/inwardly). The whole segment therefore sets the scene: “all, down (prostrate), and within themselves…”.
segments.s400-g18: همی is a classical continuative/emphatic particle (here marking an ongoing or emphatic action). از جگرشان is a causal/partitive phrase “from their jigar” (جگر used metaphorically = seat of feeling/heart). بجوشید is the past tense of جوشیدن (‘boiled, seethed’); in Persian poetry the verb can precede its subject, so خون (blood) follows as the thing that boiled. Literally: “indeed/from their hearts seethed (the) blood.”
segments.s400-g19: به دژ is a locative phrase “at the fortress” (به = at/to; دژ = fortress). چون is the temporal conjunction “when.” خبر شد is the impersonal/agentless past “it became known / news came”; that is followed by the subordinate clause introduced by که: که آمد سپاه (“that the army had come”), where آمد (verb) precedes سپاه (subject) in poetic inversion.
segments.s400-g20: جهان نیست پیدا is an inverted predication: جهان (the world) + نیست پیدا (is not visible / cannot be seen) — note the adjective پیداست placed after the negative. ز introduces the cause (“because of / from”) and گرد سیاه = “black dust” (گرد = dust; سیاه = black), so the clause means the world was obscured by black dust.
segments.s400-g21: همه دژ = “the whole/entire fortress” (همه here = entire). پر از is the adjectival phrase “full of” (پر = full; از = of). نام اسفندیار is the noun phrase “the name/reputation of Esfandiyar” (نام here emphasises fame or renown). Together: “the entire fortress was filled with the name of Esfandiyar.”
segments.s400-g22: درخت بلا is a genitive/nominal compound “the tree of calamity/affliction.” حنظل آورد بار is a verbal clause with object-before-verb order: حنظل (colocynth, a bitter fruit) as the thing, آورد (brought/bore) the verb in past tense, and بار (load/fruit/burden) as the resulting ‘load’/product. Poetically: “the tree of calamity bore the bitter colocynth.”
segments.s400-g23: بپوشید is the past (3rd person) of پوشیدن “to put on / clothe / cover.” The subject ارجاسپ (Arjasp) appears after the verb by poetic inversion. خفتان جنگ follows as the object phrase; خفتان, a substantivized/archaic verbal form from خفتن ‘to sleep,’ functions here as a nominal phrase combined with جنگ (‘war’) — in context it denotes the war‑garments/war‑apparatus or the war‑band being clothed (a compact poetic phrasing).
segments.s400-g24: بمالید is the past of مالیدن ‘to rub / strike / anoint’; the agent is omitted and understood from the previous clause (Arjasp). بر is the preposition “on/upon.” چنگ (here) is the musical instrument “harp,” and the doubled phrase بسیار چنگ (literally “many harp”) is a poetic intensification meaning he plucked/struck many harps or struck the harp many times. The verb thus governs the following prepositional phrase and elided object.
segments.s400-g41: جز اسفندیار is an exception phrase placed at the start ('except Esfandiyar'). تهم را is the definite object (را marks تهم = 'reproach, slander'), and نماند is the past 3rd-person singular of ماندن meaning 'remained / was left' — together: 'there remained no reproach except from Esfandiyar.' Word order is the poetic, fronted exception followed by the verbal predicate.
segments.s400-g42: کس ... نخواند is a negative/generalizing clause: کس = 'no one' and نخواند here means 'called' / 'addressed' (past tense). او را is the object 'him' (را marks it). به جز شاه ایران is an intervening exceptive phrase 'except the Shah of Iran' — literal order is 'No one called him, except the Shah of Iran.'
segments.s400-g43: سپه (the army) is the subject; میسره میمنه is a paired phrase (left/right or the two wings/sides) describing the banners or wings, and برکشید is the past tense 'raised / lifted'. In poetic word order the modifiers follow the subject; the verb برکشید agrees with سپه as the action performed.
segments.s400-g44: چنان شد که introduces a result clause: چنان شد = 'it became/turned out so' and که introduces the content/result. کس روز روشن ندید is a negative/generalizing statement 'none (کس) saw (ندید) in the clear daylight (روز روشن)', i.e. 'no one had seen such a thing in broad daylight.' شد is simple past 'became/happened'.
segments.s400-g45: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; زخم is 'wound'; سنانهای is the plural construct 'of the spears' (سنان + های); الماس گون is a postpositive adjective phrase 'diamond-like' (الماس 'diamond' + گون suffix meaning '-like'). Together: 'from the diamond-like wounds of the spears'.
segments.s400-g46: تو is the pronoun 'you' and گفتی here functions as a reporting verb ('you said') introducing what follows. همی + present-form (بارد) is a classical/historic continuousive construction meaning 'keeps raining/falling' (here poetic: 'kept pouring'). از ابر خون is the source phrase 'from a cloud of blood' (از = 'from', ابر = 'cloud', خون = 'blood'). Note: some listed lexical senses for گفتی and بارد in the checklist do not show the verbal meanings used here, so those are marked separately.
segments.s400-g47: به جنگ اندر آمد سپاه is inverted poetic order: به جنگ اندر آمد = 'entered/ came into the battle' and سپاه is the subject 'the army'. از دو روی is a circumstantial phrase 'from two sides' (روی here = 'side'). آمد is the past tense 'came/entered'.
segments.s400-g48: هرانکس که is a relative/generalizing clause 'whoever; whoever it was that…' followed by predicative adjectives. بدگرد and پرخاشجوی are coordinated descriptors: بدگرد = 'ill-disposed / ill-tempered' (predicative, no copula required in Persian poetry) and پرخاشجوی = 'quarrelsome / aggressive'; و links them. The clause is elliptical/characteristic: 'whoever was ill-disposed and quarrelsome...' (continues after this line).
segments.s400-g49: بشد is an epic/past form meaning 'he went/advanced' (third person). The prepositional phrase پیش نوش‌آذر follows the verb and means 'into the presence of / before Nowsh‑Azar'; Persian allows the subject (تیغ‌زن, 'the swordsman') to come after the verb and its adjuncts, so تیغ‌زن is the agent who advanced.
segments.s400-g50: همی is a habitual/progressive particle used with past verbs in classical Persian (marks continuing or emphatic action). جست is the past of 'to leap/spring'; پرخاش here denotes a physical assault/attack, and زان (an archaic contraction of از آن) indicates 'at/from that', with انجمن 'assembly' the target. The subject is omitted (carried over from the previous clause).
segments.s400-g51: بیامد is simple past 'came'. سرافراز is an adjective 'triumphant / lofty' modifying طرخان (here used as a proper name/title). برش in this position is an archaic/fused element (بر + ش) meaning 'upon him / against him' (a cliticized preposition + pronoun); this form is idiomatic in older Persian.
segments.s400-g52: که introduces a purposive/result clause here ('so that' / 'with the result that'). از تن به خاک اندر آرد is literal word order 'bring/from the body into the earth (he) brings' where آرد is the present/epic-form of آوردن used in the subordinate purpose clause ('that he might bring'). سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش 'his').
segments.s400-g53: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. نوش‌آذر is the subject; او را is the object pronoun with the object marker را (here 'him' as definite object). به هامون marks location 'on/onto the plain'. بدید is the past tense 'saw'.
segments.s400-g54: بزد is past 'he struck' (here 'he struck with his hand'); دست names the instrument/body-part. The coordinating و links the two actions; تیغ از میان برکشید is a verb phrase 'drew (lit. 'lifted') the sword from between' where برکشید is the past of 'to draw/raise' (commonly used for drawing a weapon) and از میان specifies 'from out of / from between'.
segments.s400-g55: کمرگاه طرخان is the noun phrase 'the Tarkhan's waist/loin' (possessive order: noun followed by the possessor). بدو here is an archaic adverbial/clitic meaning 'upon him / at him'; نیم 'half' combines with کرد 'made/did' to express 'made into halves' — together 'نیم کرد' = 'he cut in two'. The verb کرد is simple past 'did/made' and yields the meaning 'he severed the Tarkhan at the waist.'
segments.s400-g56: دل کهرم = 'the (aged) heart' (کهرم here functions adjectivally/epithetically, 'old/aged'). از درد marks the cause 'from pain'. پربیم is a compound adjective 'full of fear/fearful'; کرد (again past 'made') yields the resultive construction 'made (his heart) full of fear' — i.e. 'his old heart was filled with fearful pain.'
segments.s400-g33: سرافراز (adjective) precedes the title طرخان and qualifies it as 'proud/illustrious'; طرخان is a title/name. بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came', and دوان is an adverbial participle/verb-form placed after the verb to indicate manner ('rushing' / 'galloping'), so بیامد دوان = 'came rushing.'
segments.s400-g34: بدین روی is an idiomatic locution meaning 'thus / in this manner / in this direction' (ب + دین/روی fused in the classical text). دژ is the noun 'fortress'. با introduces an accompanying noun phrase and یکی ترجمان = 'a certain interpreter' (یکی used indeterminately). The whole phrase means 'thus toward/at the fortress with a certain interpreter.'
segments.s400-g35: سپه here 'army' is the noun seen; دید is the simple past 'saw' (the subject 'he' is omitted). با introduces accompaniment: جوشن 'cuirass/armor' and ساز جنگ 'instruments/devices of war' describe the army's accoutrements. The prepositional phrase modifies how the army appeared.
segments.s400-g36: درفشی 'standard/banner (or its bearer)' is qualified by the compound adjective سیه پیکر 'black-bodied' (سیه + پیکر). او is a pronominal subject 'he/that one' and is followed by پلنگ which serves as a nominal predicate identifying him: the clause has an implied copula (بود) — 'the banner-bearer, black-bodied, was a leopard.'
segments.s400-g37: سپه‌کش is an epithet 'army-slayer' attached to the proper name پشوتن; the sequence names the warrior. به قلب اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the inner heart' (into the centre/heart of the formation), indicating the direction or target of his action — the verb of striking is implicit from the epic context.
segments.s400-g38: سپاهی همه 'the whole army' is the noun phrase followed by the predicate دست شسته 'hands washed' (shسته is a past participle used predicatively, the copula is omitted). به خون marks the material/agent 'in blood' — together: 'the entire army had hands washed in blood' (i.e. their hands were bloodied).
segments.s400-g39: به introduces direction 'into / toward' and چنگ here denotes a grip/hold or figuratively 'the midst/hand' (classical usage), so به چنگ اندرون = 'into the inner grasp / into the inner ranks.' گرز اسفندیار places the weapon and its owner together: 'the mace of Esfandiyar' (possessive relation expressed by juxtaposition, a common epic pattern).
segments.s400-g40: به زیر اندرون is a locative/directional phrase 'down under/into the lower inner part.' باره here (poetic usage) functions as 'a stroke/blow/assault' (an instance of action) and نامدار qualifies it as 'renowned/famed.' The clause lacks an explicit copula or main verb and so reads as a compressed report: 'a famed downward blow (was delivered) into the inner part.'

sentences.s400-s37: He told all the Turks, “Get out — leave the fortress and go straight to the plain.”
sentences.s400-s38: Bring the entire army into the middle; let forth a mighty, life-giving cry.
sentences.s400-s39: Let not a single one of them remain alive; likewise let no one call their names.
sentences.s400-s40: The whole army came out of the fortress onto the road; they came with weary hearts and as avengers.
sentences.s400-s13: He commanded that Kahram, the lion‑catcher, take the army and the war‑drum, the sword, and the arrow.
sentences.s400-s14: He said to Tarkhan thus: 'O exalted one, go swiftly with an army prepared for battle.'
sentences.s400-s15: Seize the Namdaran fortress with ten thousand men; all are battle‑seeking, dagger‑bearing fighters.
sentences.s400-s16: Watch what these warriors are, and how, by that heavy charge, they contrive to clear (the way/foes) away.
sentences.s400-s5: Peshotan thus said that, compared with the elephant and the lion, the brave man is more given to sloth.
sentences.s400-s6: That his eye be far from his body; may all his days be a feast (for him).
sentences.s400-s7: He struck the iron ney and the iron jar; from the gate rose the lowing/cry of the cattle.
sentences.s400-s8: From the plain toward the fortress came an army; the host loomed dark against the blazing sun.
sentences.s400-s29: Thus he also launched an attack into the heart of the host; one of his great men was a man of wisdom.
sentences.s400-s30: Like chargers the two hosts crashed into one another, and a cloud of arrows closed over their heads.
sentences.s400-s31: Triumphant Kahrum went toward the fortress; fleeing, he went, and the army pressed on hotly.
sentences.s400-s32: Thus Kahrum said before his father, 'O renowned king Khurshidfar!'
sentences.s400-s33: A great army came from Iran into the inner presence, famed for mighty renown.
sentences.s400-s34: Esfandiar is lofty and honored — and that is enough; no one may (or can) enter this fortress.
sentences.s400-s35: That very spear is a weapon of war in its grip — the one you saw at the Gonbadān fortress in battle.
sentences.s400-s36: Arjasp's heart was filled with sorrow from that speech, for the old hatred was newly revived once more.
sentences.s400-s1: Night came; someone kindled a fire whose spitting blaze even burned the sky.
sentences.s400-s2: When the watchman looked from the lookout, he perceived night as fire and day as smoky.
sentences.s400-s3: From a place he returned in ill spirits; you said that he became a companion (or was allied) with the wind.
sentences.s400-s4: When (he) came from the road to Peshotan, he said what he had seen of fire and smoke.
sentences.s400-s9: All fell down, and within themselves blood boiled from their hearts.
sentences.s400-s10: When news reached the fortress that the army had come, the world could not be seen for the black dust.
sentences.s400-s11: The whole fortress was full of Esfandiyar’s name; the tree of calamity bore the bitter colocynth.
sentences.s400-s12: Arjasp put on the garments of war; he struck/plucked many a harp.
sentences.s400-s21: No one remained to reproach him except Esfandiyar; no one called him except the Shah of Iran.
sentences.s400-s22: The army raised its left and right standards; it became such that none had seen the like in broad daylight.
sentences.s400-s23: From the diamond-like wounds of the spears — you said — blood kept pouring from the cloud.
sentences.s400-s24: The army entered the battle from two sides; whoever was ill-disposed and quarrelsome...
sentences.s400-s25: The swordsman rushed forward into Nowsh‑Azar's presence and leapt at that assembly in assault.
sentences.s400-s26: The triumphant Tarkhan came at him, intending to bring his head from his body to the earth.
sentences.s400-s27: When Nowsh‑Azar saw him on the plain, he struck with his hand and drew his sword from between (its sheath).
sentences.s400-s28: Nowsh‑Azar cleft the Tarkhan's waist in two; the Tarkhan's aged heart, from the pain, became full of fear.
sentences.s400-s17: The proud Tarkhan came rushing, and thus approached the fortress with a certain interpreter.
sentences.s400-s18: He beheld an army, with cuirasses and war-gear; its banner-bearer, black-bodied, was a leopard.
sentences.s400-s19: Peshotan the army‑slayer struck into the inner heart; all the soldiers had their hands washed in blood.
sentences.s400-s20: Esfandiyar's mace struck into the inner ranks, delivering a famed downward blow.
